sentences.text: I am, yes.
I do.
Every game I watch.
Yeah, I've seen several.
Tomorrow, [INAUDIBLE].
Tomorrow, two games.
Yeah.
I have a friend.
I think a friend's going to one of the games.
[TONE] ?] So I guess Brazil's going to win.
You think?
Since Germany's gone.
Siegfried Halbreich, and it is the 12th of July.
Nordhausen.
Yeah.
OK.
Sure.
When we arrived in Nordhausen, we were brought up to the main camp, and we stayed there about a week.
Other transports [? formed ?], [? and ?] they took us down, about 800 prisoners, to this city Nordhausen.
This is just down a march of three miles.
When we came to the city, there was a camp out of the city, a former military camp.
They housed there the Air Force.
They have huge halls where they put the planes in there.
And they opened there a hospital.
When we came, marched down, the SS men asked who worked in the hospital.
I stepped out.
What were you doing?
I explained to him.
And he said, from now on, you take over the hospital.
You have other people who worked in the hospital-- there was not one.
But they were friends.
And I picked out maybe up to eight people.
I said, this one, this one, this one.
Later on, I had hard trouble because-- had trouble with them because they didn't know what to do.
So I had to go from room with room with help them, you know?
And bandage or something.
They had no idea.
But there were nurses.
I saved them, again.
But instead, after the roll call in the evening, all the people went to the barracks, entered, got some food and [INAUDIBLE].
But I said, no.
I want to see what's going on.
When I went upstairs to the hospital, there was again-- I say ambulance-- an emergency room, where people came from work and wanted to be treated.
Before they even go to eat or to the barracks, they want to be treated.
And I go up in this room quite a lot.
It's as large as, maybe, this room here.
And there was a doctor, a young doctor.
And the people came up.
And he started to treat them, hit them in the face, kick them around.
Oh, you dirty Jew.
You will not anyhow live long.
You will finish up here.
What do you come here to be treated?
And I look and I look about 10, 12 minutes.
I just couldn't take it anymore.
I called him, and I said, tell me.
What are you doing here?
Oh, I'm the doctor-- in Polish.
He's a Polish doctor.
And I said, oh, you are the doctor?
Where did you go to medical school?
At the Warsaw University.
I said, you know, I cannot believe that in the Warsaw University they taught you this kind of medicine.
And don't you realize what you're doing?
You're a prisoner like we.
And you treat your brothers that suffer like you this way?
For whom do you work for?
The Germans?
Believe me-- you will finish up like all other ones, like we all.
And from now on, I don't want to see this kind of treatment.
And he went back and he changed.
After it was through, I wanted to walk out, and another prisoner comes to me.
And in Polish, he would like to talk to me.
I said, fine, OK.
What are you doing?
Oh, I'm here the secretary.
And he registered the people if they should stay in the hospital or being-- getting aspirin or something, you know?
He [? treated. ?] I said, oh, I see.
What do you do?
And he explained to me.
I said, tell me.
From where are you?
I'm from Katowice, my hometown.
I said what did you do there?
I was a [? procurator ?]-- a prosecutor-- in court.
Oh, I see.
And what did you do?
I  said I was pharmacy.
I gave him the name of the streets and where I worked.
He said [INAUDIBLE].
He said, I have to make-- I have to tell you something.
I heard your conversation with the doctor.
And I was so taken by it that I make your a proposition.
I would like to cooperate with you and help people.
I said, what did-- yeah.
He said, I have to admit something.
I was a leader in the [INAUDIBLE].
This was the anti-Semitic organization in Poland-- the leader in our state.
But he said, I changed myself.
I want to work with you.
I said, fine.
I shook [? hands ?] him, and we were the best friends.
And not only then-- we worked together in the American war crimes [INAUDIBLE] because he was a lawyer.
And afterwards, I went to the United States.
He went back home because his mother and his wife were alive.
Otherwise, he would have come to the United States.
And I saw him often.
I kept in touch with him.
I helped him with packages, with money.
I visited them seven times.
Sorry to say, last time in October when I-- last October I was there, he died.
I didn't see him anymore.
But a wonderful man.
A very pious man-- Catholic.
Every day he went to mass in the morning.
And we were living together in Wiesbaden in Germany.
He-- Friday night, I didn't [INAUDIBLE] but what I knew by heart.
I stood by the window, you know?
And I played.
And he then remembered the melodies.
And every time I came to Poland, he sang for me the Hebrew songs-- the melodies only.
One time, I was in Poland, and it was Friday night.
And he said, Sieg, do you want to go to service?
I said, Vincente, I'm not-- I'm not a Catholic.
I'm a Jew.
He said, I know.
But there is a little place in Krakow where they assemble and they play on Friday and Sunday.
I said, sure, I want to go.
And we went by streetcar there.
It was a little, tiny synagogue next to the cemetery.
And in this cemetery, the Germans demolished everything.
They left one grave of this famous rabbi, [INAUDIBLE]-- very famous rabbi.
And they even guarded it all the years, the Germans, that it shouldn't be destroyed.
And there, I came in.
A few old Jews sitting there, books thrown around, and the whole place, dusty.
And they asked me I should lead the players.
So Friday night, I played for them, you know?
This was part of it.
Now, coming back to-- Nordhausen.
Nordhausen.
Next day, I took over, naturally.
And there, I had over the 4,000 prisoners laying on tuberculosis.
Sick.
What you doing?
It's tuberculosis.
No medications, nothing.
Hardly food.
It was very meager already.
The Germans didn't have to eat.
Once in a while, we got two, three potatoes, and a little watery soup.
This was all.
But I knew how to organize.
I got more for other people, for my friends, and so far.
And even the SS men were eating with me.
And one day, all of a sudden, who comes?
The administrator from Auschwitz was sent to our camp.
I was very friendly with him.
He said, Sieg, maybe you could organize for me a bike.
I want to get away because the war's over.
I said, no, but you want from me a bike?
I'm already five and a half years in the camps.
From where should I get a bike?
You should get your bike yourself.
And the doctors came, make selections.
So what did you do?
The young people-- when the doctor was in one room, I took-- from the other-- from the room a hat off.
People out, and put them in the room when he already selected, you know?
Just to save people.
And he selected hundreds of people, and they died.
And we kept the bodies in the special rooms, high-- I don't know-- 10, 15 on top, you know?
We waited until the truck comes from the main camp.
And we throw them on the truck.
I have the pictures.
We throw them on the truck, and up they go through the chimney.
There was one prisoner, an Austrian doctor, who worked with me in Auschwitz already, and he came to this camp.
When he came, he took over because he was a non-Jew.
I was a Jew.
But I stayed there.
I had no function anymore, but I did my job.
And he was jealous that all the nurses, the doctors, they didn't go to him for advice or something.
They came all to me.
And he was jealous.
He, one day, went to the doctor, to the SS doctor, and reported what they do.
That I help people and I take people to other rooms, save them before the selection.
The doctor didn't say a word.
And from time or time, from our hospital, there were people put on trains.
They said they go to recuperate, but they went all to their deaths.
And there were transports.
And the doctor gives me an order.
I go with the next transport to-- in charge of the transport.
Never before was somebody sent with the transport.
They sent me.
So I knew already this is the end.
I know where we're going.
I said goodbye to my people already.
And but I had in my mind that I jump the train, because I was the only one on the train who could still jump, even kill a German, you know?
And this was in the afternoon.
And next morning, the transport's supposed to go.
And all of a sudden, it was April 3rd at 3 o'clock.
An alarm and American Air Force over us, and dropped the bombs just on our camp.
But the whole camp was entirely destroyed.
And out of this 4,000 people, we were 200 survived.
Because they died.
They were in the camps, hanging in the ceiling, dead bodies.
And it was awful.
It was burning days and days.
We were still [INAUDIBLE] the nurses and doctors, the administration people, [INAUDIBLE].
We [? were ?] on [? our ?] way.
And the Americans made a mistake because they didn't know this is a concentration camp.
They knew it as a military camp.
They emptied the military months before.
In January, [? they ?] put us in, you see?
So they didn't know.
So they came back at 9 o'clock in the morning and hit the whole city.
The whole city they flattened.
We ran away in the-- in the-- in the fields.
And when they emptied the planes, they came and [INAUDIBLE] us with machine guns [INAUDIBLE].
They couldn't know who it is.
This was Germans, you know?
And we went into the woods, and we remained about a week in the woods.
And we ate only the raw potatoes, or how you call this fruit-- this yellow fruit.
We called it [?
[POLISH]. ?] You give this fruit to horses to eat.
And we ate it, you know?
And we saw sometimes SS men still going and catching prisoners and [? taking ?] [? them ?] [? yet ?], you know?
It was the end of the war.
They were already surrounded from all sides, but they all still didn't give up.
They didn't give up.
I returned to the city, to the camp.
And here, my friend, the one from Krakov who died-- oh, Sieg, we are looking for you.
Why?
Listen-- you speak English.
We cannot communicate with the Americans.
We don't know what they want, but we want to tell them something, to ask questions.
We can't.
So I right away started to work with American war crimes.
And here, two days after working-- I worked right away.
I didn't go to a DP camp anymore because I was right away in their hotel, in the city with offices.
They wanted to give me a uniform.
I said, no uniform.
I had enough.
I had the Polish uniform.
I had 5 and 1/2 years the concentration camp uniform.
I don't wear any uniform.
And we both were civilian employees, but we lived in the office's hotel-- the best hotel-- with the food-- the best hotel, you know?
We had everything.
You were formally liberated when?
When?
I would say it was April [INAUDIBLE] we were bombarded about 10 days later.
About 14th, 15th of April.
By the Americans, yeah, sure.
And then I began right away work with American War Crimes Commission.
First of all, I was hired as an interpreter and as an investigator because of the languages, you see?
I went with the, as we say, Americans in the Jeeps, you know, from camp to camp.
And we emptied the camp.
First of all, the Americans didn't even know how to start.
They were only interested to catch as many SS men as possible.
So we said to them, how do you recognize an SS man?
He was never in the army.
He was at home or he was there and there.
He will lie to you.
How do we recognize?
I said, tell him to pick up the arm.
Because every SS man, like we have our arms here, the number, every SS man has a number in their armpit.
See?
This was one thing.
I said, how about other things?
Why don't we collect records And [? there's ?] all camps all over.
There were hundreds of camps.
We don't need them.
I said, what do you mean you don't need them?
No, we have the SS men and that's enough.
I said, please, give us the permission to collect it.
So we took trucks with their permission, and we went from camp to camp.
And every little paper, slip of paper, we collected.
And every [INAUDIBLE], SS men had their names, you know, with their records.
The Germans kept beautiful records.
And this was their down path, you know?
They lost the war because of the records.
We have all the records.
Every-- Every SS man, every prisoner-- they were all recorded.
I show you records I have seen here.
And so we took it.
And this way, they were able to make the trials.
And we collected it for about one-- then [INAUDIBLE] from Nordhausen, we were transported to [INAUDIBLE].
This was in Western Germany-- the whole unit there.
And there we remained only maybe two weeks.
And then we went back to Wiesbaden.
This is near Frankfurt.
And this was the headquarter.
And there we stayed up to the trial of Nuremberg.
This was it.
I got my papers.
And yeah, then during the time I was working, two days after [? the ?] liberation, my captain said, Sieg, take the Jeep, go up to the displaced person camp.
The concentration camp was immediately changed to a displaced person camp.
Because what do you do with the tens of thousand people?
Where do they go?
There was no communication, no railroad, no post, no telephone, nothing.
So what did you do?
They opened displaced persons camps, and they flooded them with food from America, from England, you know?
And people, so many people, died from overeating.
They got sick.
They couldn't take it.
And I begged them, please, eat slowly.
You are like a baby.
First, the mother milk, and then you get the cereal.
You didn't eat for four, five years this kind of food.
You will get sick.
Eat slowly.
You don't have to worry about tomorrow because there's much left here yet.
You will have it [? forever. ?] No, the [? eyes ?] ate [? everything. ?] And my friend in Poland, this way too-- he over-- he was so fat.
But I kept-- I didn't eat.
I ate normally every meal.
This was it.
And then two days after, he sent me upstairs.
I'm coming upstairs to DP camp.
I find a friend from Poland, a nurse from Auschwitz.
Oh, Sieg, how are you?
I'm glad you're alive.
What are you doing?
I said, I work for some Americans.
He said, why don't you stay overnight here?
We have a little party.
Can you imagine?
Two days after liberation, the prisoners, they partied already.
Food was there, everything, you know?
But I said, OK, I will stay.
I [? stopped ?] a soldier there.
And the GI go down and tomorrow, pick me up in the morning.
And we're sitting in a room, and benches around.
And here next to me, two young girls, Polish girls.
And we start to talk.
From where are you?
What are you doing?
What you have in mind to do?
And after a while, I ask, tell me, are you Jewish?
She jumps at me-- how dare you to ask me this kind of a question?
I said, you know, quiet down.
We are liberated.
We are free.
You have nothing to worry anymore.
You should be proud that you are Jewish and you survived.
So she admitted.
And this is my wife.
She was with another girl, the younger girl.
And I took them in the evening to the barracks.
And I stayed with other friends of them.
Next morning, the soldier picked me up and I went down.
I'm coming down, I see they're preparing a transport to France.
French prisoners, concentration camps, they were sent back to France.
So I said to the captain, you know, I have two young girls upstairs in the DP camp.
They don't know where to go.
They cannot go to Poland.
They don't know if they have relatives, who is alive.
Can they go with them to France?
He said, sure.
I went back with [INAUDIBLE] up.
I said, look, girls.
This is it.
We have a choice.
You want to stay here, or you want to go to France?
And France is liberated already for half a year.
And there, you have people there.
Yeah, we go with.
I took them down.
And next day, they went to the transport.
I never heard for a long time what happened to them.
All of a sudden, I get the call.
Because in France, there was telephone.
It started already but not in Germany.
But I was working for the American army.
We had our telephone, military telephone.
She called me.
And we called quite often.
She called me, I called her.
And October '44-- '45, I took furlough, but they wouldn't give it to me.
I had to go only as a serviceman for investigation, interrogation in France.
So they gave me five days.
I came to Paris.
I met her.
And she was the only of all the displaced people who worked.
All other ones were sitting in the Rothschild house and getting the food and sleeping and having a good time.
And she worked.
She went to a shop, and she was the cutter for the clothes-- for clothes-- in order to make money.
It was a Jewish outfit.
And this impressed me very much.
And we had a good time the five days, and I went back.
Christmas, I went back again, and we decided we get married.
But what to do?
She's in France, I'm in German.
I have my affidavit from the United States, from here, from Germany.
I found a German-- a Polish officer, and I asked him if he could bring my fiance to Germany.
And this was strictly prohibited to travel from one country to another one.
This was occupied by Germans, by-- no, by British, by French, and by Italians, you see?
By French.
And I'm waiting, I'm waiting.
One day, another day.
Weeks passed.
All of sudden, a friend of her calls me-- Sieg, Ruth is already on the way.
I go to the station.
I wait from one train to another coming from Paris.
Ruth isn't here.
I gave up at 12 o'clock.
I gave up.
I go to the hotel, and by the door, by the entrance, the guard tells me, oh, Mr.
Harper, your wife is already here.
I go up.
She is in bed after the shower already, you know.
I said, how did you make it?
She went by the train with the officer.
And they came to the German border, and the Germans controlled the train.
And he didn't have papers for her.
He just took her, you know?
He got cold feet.
He turned back to Paris and left her on the border.
And the Germans took her out on the station.
She was sitting hours and hours.
And this American soldier comes-- what are you doing here?
And she started to cry and said, I wanted to go to Germany, to Wiesbaden, because my husband works for the American army, and he is sick, and I have to visit him.
I have to see him.
He said, what are you doing here?
Well, the Germans wouldn't let me go.
Oh, they're crazy.
Go on the next train.
And she went on next thing and came somewhere around, you know?
I missed the train.
And then here, she was there.
This is-- When did you and your wife come to the states?
We came-- we remained till June, '46.
She came about in February, '46 from Paris.
And I still worked with the American war crimes, you know?
And then we got an apartment.
The Germans had to give me right away an apartment because I couldn't live in the hotel with the officers and with my friend, you know?
We got an apartment.
And we lived separate.
And I went every day to work.
And here, we have to go to Germany-- to America.
We have no money.
Sieg.
We will go without money.
What will we do?
I got a salary from the Americans.
And my money, I sent every month to Cleveland to my cousin through the APO through the military [INAUDIBLE] mail-- post.
And I kept here a few thousand dollars.
So I said, we have no money.
I will do something.
I would go into the black market.
And all of my friends, they became millionaires.
They did black market in Germany, smuggling cigarettes, liquor, and all those things, you know?
They became millionaires.
So I never wanted to do it-- never.
I said, I don't need it.
Because I thought I come to the United States, I will go in my profession, and I will work as a pharmacist.
I don't do this kind of work.
She said, we have to do it, she said.
So I went every morning to work.
And I [? sold ?] to all the officers-- do you need some money?
Yes.
They gave me dollars.
I took it-- the lunchtime route, went to the DP camp, displaced person camp, near Frankfurt.
The name was [INAUDIBLE].
And in the afternoon, she brought money.
She changed the money.
She got, for $1, 200 marks.
But I gave the officers only 100.
And we-- quite a lot.
In this few months, we saved some money.
And this was it.
And I sent it all to the United States.
This picture is from 1972.
There was a visit of my aunt and uncle for first time from the United States.
This is my youngest brother.
This is my aunt from the United States.
This is the uncle from the United States-- no, this is my grandmother.
This is the uncle of the United-- from the United States.
This is my mother.
This is my aunt and this is my aunt.
My mother's name was Amelia.
My brother's name was Paul.
The children-- there is-- the last-- the first one here is my sister, Katie.
This is a cousin.
This is a cousin and this is a cousin.
This is me in the Polish army.
I served in 1931-- no, 1930 and '31 in the Polish army in the officer school.
This is my older brother, Isador, and this is myself.
At age 2 and 1/2, 3 and 1/2.
I don't know exactly.
This is my older brother, Isador.
This is my younger brother, Paul, the first day in school.
This is my younger brother, Paul.
This is my sister, Katie.
This is my older brother, Isador-- no, I'm sorry.
This is myself, and this is my older brother, Isador.
This is after the war in 1945.
This is myself, my wife, Ruth, and an American soldier friend of ours.
We met, naturally, after the war.
